steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Transitions question. The first sentence makes a broad claim: Singh worked at JPL. The second sentence narrows to a specific example of that work: her role on the Perseverance rover team. The transition must signal a move from general to specific.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Conversely,: signals contrast or opposition between two ideas. Singh's rover work is not opposite to or in conflict with her working at JPL; it is a subset of it. ✗
  • Choice B — Specifically,: signals that what follows is a more precise version or focused example of what came before. Singh's Perseverance role is exactly that — a specific instance of her JPL work. ✓
  • Choice C — Finally,: signals the last item in a list or the end of a sequence. The passage is not enumerating a series of Singh's roles; it moves from one general statement to one specific one. ✗
  • Choice D — Nevertheless,: signals concession — something is true despite an obstacle. There is no obstacle or concession anywhere in the passage. ✗
That confirms B is correct.
